perturbation.original_text: 2. Definition. "Confidential Information" means any information, ---------- technical data, or know-how, including, but not limited to that which relates to research, products, software, services, development, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, formulas, engineering, marketing, finances, financial models, and business plans, which Confidential Information is designated in writing to be confidential or proprietary, or if given orally, is confirmed promptly in writing as having been disclosed as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definition. "Confidential Information" means any information, ---------- technical data, or know-how, including, but not limited to that which relates to research, products, software, services, development, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, formulas, engineering, marketing, finances, financial models, and business plans. Information is considered confidential regardless of written designation or oral confirmation.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for written designation or oral confirmation for information to be considered confidential, the contract broadens the definition beyond what is legally defensible as a trade secret. Under California law, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are required.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts CUTSA because it eliminates the requirement for the information holder to actively designate or confirm information as confidential. CUTSA requires a showing that the information is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy, which is undermined by automatically classifying all information as confidential.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy as it broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the need for written designation or oral confirmation. The original text aligns with the concept of "duty to maintain secrecy" mentioned in CIV 3426.1(a), which the modified text undermines by automatically classifying all information as confidential, regardless of any effort to keep it secret. This means information that wouldn't be protected as a trade secret due to lack of effort to maintain its secrecy is now contractually considered confidential, potentially conflicting with trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information. Each party agrees ------------------------------------------ not to use the Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other party for its own use or for any purpose except to carry out discussions concerning the completion of any business relationship between the two. Each party will not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party to third parties or to its employees except employees who are required to have the information in order to carry out the contemplated business.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information. Each party agrees ------------------------------------------ not to use the Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other party for its own use or for any purpose except to carry out discussions concerning the completion of any business relationship between the two. Each party will not disclose the Confidential Information of the other party to third parties, including its employees, unless authorized by a court order.
perturbation.explanation: This modification unduly restricts the disclosure of confidential information, even to employees who require it for business purposes, unless a court order is obtained. This is an unreasonably high standard and could conflict with the 'need to know' principle in trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts CUTSA by making it virtually impossible for a business to operate normally because it can't share information with its staff. Employees are often critical for trade secret activities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by unduly restricting the disclosure of confidential information, even to employees who require it for business purposes, unless a court order is obtained. This conflicts with the normal operation of a business and could hinder trade secret activities, as the Law Explanation notes. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, which cites California's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), supports the argument that restricting access to confidential information to employees performing necessary tasks is an unreasonable restriction, potentially undermining the purpose of trade secret protection. The change introduces a high legal bar (court order) that would likely fail legal scrutiny and nullify required standards for employees needing access to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it would be difficult to measure the damage to such party from the breach of the other party's obligations hereunder, that injury to such party from any such breach would be impossible to calculate, and that monetary damages would therefor be an inadequate remedy; accordingly, each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled, in addition to all other remedies it might have, to injunctions or other appropriate orders to restrain any such breach without showing or proving any actual damage.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it would be difficult to measure the damage to such party from the breach of the other party's obligations hereunder. Therefore, in the event of a breach, the breaching party will only be liable for a flat fee of $100, regardless of actual damages.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the contract to limit damages to a nominal amount, effectively precluding meaningful remedies for breach of confidentiality. This could be deemed unconscionable under California law, especially if the actual damages far exceed this amount.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text may violate California Civil Code Section 1670 if the liquidated damages clause is deemed unreasonable under the circumstances existing at the time the contract was made. The code makes void a contract provision fixing damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670. Any dispute arising from a construction contract with a public agency, which contract contains a provision that one party to the contract or one party’s agent or employee shall decide any disputes arising under that contract, shall be resolved by submitting the dispute to independent arbitration, if mutually agreeable, otherwise by litigation in a court of competent jurisdiction. (Added by Stats. 1978, Ch. 1374.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle that fixed damage clauses are void if deemed unreasonable. The original text allowed for injunctions and other remedies, while the changed text limits damages to a flat fee, potentially violating California Civil Code Section 1670, as explained in the Law Explanation. The single scraped law snippet does not directly address fixed damages but sets the stage of the legal article that would have followed that section. The perturbation explanation indicated that such a term could be considered unconcsionable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to immediately notify the other party in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information of the other party which may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to notify the other party of any misuse of confidential information, but the notification does not need to be in writing and can be delayed up to 6 months after discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying notification and removing the written requirement for reporting misuse or misappropriation of confidential information weakens the protection of trade secrets and can hinder timely legal action. This contradicts the need for prompt action in trade secret cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't explicitly dictate notification timing, Section 3426.6 addresses statutes of limitations and a delayed notification could impact the ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation within the statutory time frame. Prompt notification is important for remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.6. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.6. An action for misappropriation must be brought within three years after the misappropriation is discovered or by the exercise of reasonable diligence should have been discovered. For the purposes of this section, a continuing misappropriation constitutes a single claim. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by delaying the notification period for misuse of confidential information up to 6 months and removing the written requirement. California Code, CIV 3426.6 states that 'An action for misappropriation must be brought within three years after the misappropriation is discovered or by the exercise of reasonable diligence should have been discovered.' By delaying notification, the changed text could impede the ability to bring an action within the three-year statute of limitations, as the discovery of the misappropriation is delayed.
